short_excerpt: Pydantic attempts to provide useful validation errors. Below are details on common validation errors users may encounter when working with pydantic, together with some suggestions on how to fix them.
text: Pydantic attempts to provide useful validation errors. Below are details on common validation errors users may encounter when working with pydantic, together with some suggestions on how to fix them.

short_excerpt: arguments_type
text: arguments_type

short_excerpt: This error is raised when an object that would be passed as arguments to a function during validation is not a tuple , list , or dict . Because NamedTuple uses function calls in its implementation, that is one way to produce this error:
text: This error is raised when an object that would be passed as arguments to a function during validation is not a tuple , list , or dict . Because NamedTuple uses function calls in its implementation, that is one way to produce this error:

short_excerpt: from typing import NamedTuple from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError class MyNamedTuple(NamedTuple): x: int class MyModel(BaseModel): field: MyNamedTuple try: MyModel.model_validate({'field': 'invalid'}) except ValidationError as exc: print(repr(exc.errors()[0]['type'])) #> 'arguments_type'
text: from typing import NamedTuple from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError class MyNamedTuple(NamedTuple): x: int class MyModel(BaseModel): field: MyNamedTuple try: MyModel.model_validate({'field': 'invalid'}) except ValidationError as exc: print(repr(exc.errors()[0]['type'])) #> 'arguments_type'

short_excerpt: assertion_error
text: assertion_error

short_excerpt: This error is raised when a failing assert statement is encountered during validation:
text: This error is raised when a failing assert statement is encountered during validation:

short_excerpt: from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError, field_validator class Model(BaseModel): x: int @field_validator('x') @classmethod def force_x_positive(cls, v): assert v > 0 return v try: Model(x=-1) except ValidationError as exc: print(repr(exc.errors()[0]['type'])) #> 'assertion_error'
text: from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError, field_validator class Model(BaseModel): x: int @field_validator('x') @classmethod def force_x_positive(cls, v): assert v > 0 return v try: Model(x=-1) except ValidationError as exc: print(repr(exc.errors()[0]['type'])) #> 'assertion_error'

short_excerpt: bytes_invalid_encoding
text: bytes_invalid_encoding

short_excerpt: This error is raised when a bytes value is invalid under the configured encoding. In the following example, 'a' is invalid hex (odd number of digits).
text: This error is raised when a bytes value is invalid under the configured encoding. In the following example, 'a' is invalid hex (odd number of digits).

short_excerpt: from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError class Model(BaseModel): x: bytes model_config = {'val_json_bytes': 'hex'} try: Model(x='a') except ValidationError as exc: print(repr(exc.errors()[0]['type'])) #> 'bytes_invalid_encoding'
text: from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError class Model(BaseModel): x: bytes model_config = {'val_json_bytes': 'hex'} try: Model(x='a') except ValidationError as exc: print(repr(exc.errors()[0]['type'])) #> 'bytes_invalid_encoding'

short_excerpt: dataclass_exact_type
text: dataclass_exact_type

short_excerpt: This error is raised when validating a dataclass with strict=True and the input is not an instance of the dataclass:
text: This error is raised when validating a dataclass with strict=True and the input is not an instance of the dataclass:

short_excerpt: import pydantic.dataclasses from pydantic import TypeAdapter, ValidationError @pydantic.dataclasses.dataclass class MyDataclass: x: str adapter = TypeAdapter(MyDataclass) print(adapter.validate_python(MyDataclass(x='test'), strict=True)) #> MyDataclass(x='test') print(adapter.validate_python({'x': 'test'})) #> MyDataclass(x='test') try: adapter.validate_python({'x': 'test'}, strict=True) except ValidationError as exc: print(repr(exc.errors()[0]['type'])) #> 'dataclass_exact_type'
text: import pydantic.dataclasses from pydantic import TypeAdapter, ValidationError @pydantic.dataclasses.dataclass class MyDataclass: x: str adapter = TypeAdapter(MyDataclass) print(adapter.validate_python(MyDataclass(x='test'), strict=True)) #> MyDataclass(x='test') print(adapter.validate_python({'x': 'test'})) #> MyDataclass(x='test') try: adapter.validate_python({'x': 'test'}, strict=True) except ValidationError as exc: print(repr(exc.errors()[0]['type'])) #> 'dataclass_exact_type'

short_excerpt: This error is raised when the input datetime value provided for a date field has a nonzero time component. For a timestamp to parse into a field of type date , the time components must all be zero:

text: from datetime import date, datetime from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError class Model(BaseModel): x: date Model(x='2023-01-01') # OK Model(x=datetime(2023, 1, 1)) # OK try: Model(x=datetime(2023, 1, 1, 12)) except ValidationError as exc: print(repr(exc.errors()[0]['type'])) #> 'date_from_datetime_inexact'

short_excerpt: default_factory_not_called

short_excerpt: This error is raised when a default factory taking validated data can’t be called, because validation failed on previous fields:
text: This error is raised when a default factory taking validated data can’t be called, because validation failed on previous fields:

short_excerpt: from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, ValidationError class Model(BaseModel): a: int = Field(gt=10) b: int = Field(default_factory=lambda data: data['a']) try: Model(a=1) except ValidationError as exc: print(exc) """ 2 validation errors for Model a Input should be greater than 10 [type=greater_than, input_value=1, input_type=int] b The default factory uses validated data, but at least one validation error occurred [type=default_factory_not_called] """ print(repr(exc.errors()[1]['type'])) #> 'default_factory_not_called'
text: from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, ValidationError class Model(BaseModel): a: int = Field(gt=10) b: int = Field(default_factory=lambda data: data['a']) try: Model(a=1) except ValidationError as exc: print(exc) """ 2 validation errors for Model a Input should be greater than 10 [type=greater_than, input_value=1, input_type=int] b The default factory uses validated data, but at least one validation error occurred [type=default_factory_not_called] """ print(repr(exc.errors()[1]['type'])) #> 'default_factory_not_called'
